1:
The chapter opens on a sickbed, and the witnesses set it at once in its dark hour. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the time: "in those days" assigns the illness in a general way to the period of the Assyrian invasion, in Hezekiah's fourteenth year, since he was promised fifteen more years yet reigned only twenty-nine in all (2 Kings 18:2), and God's pledge to defend Jerusalem still lay in the future. On this ground they reject the widespread notion that the sickness was an attack of plague connected with the pestilence in the Assyrian camp, robbing that theory of its chief support. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, agrees the malady may have been caused or aggravated by the intense anxiety of the peril, and notes that Hezekiah's hymn of thanksgiving (Isa. 38:10-20) contains no allusion to the destruction of the Assyrians, proof that he recovered before that catastrophe; the precise nature of the illness, he adds, cannot be ascertained.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the bare notice into a meditation on mortality. The historian, having shown blaspheming Sennacherib destroyed in the midst of the prospects of life, now shows praying Hezekiah delivered in the midst of the prospects of death. Neither greatness nor goodness can exempt a man from sickness; Hezekiah, lately favoured of heaven above most men, is seized in the midst of his days, under forty, so that "in the midst of life we are in death," and we must always rejoice with trembling. Henry does read the disease as the plague, pointing to the boil or plague-sore of v.7: the same stroke that was killing to the Assyrians was trying to their king. The warning to prepare is brought by Isaiah, twice before a messenger of good tidings, now the bearer of a hard word, that the disease is mortal, and Hezekiah must with all speed set his house in order.

On that charge the wesleyan-arminian voices are practical. Adam Clarke reads "set thine house in order" as a command to make his will and dispose of his affairs, since without a miracle the disorder must end in death; and he presses the same chronology, fourteen years before the sickness and the fifteen granted after making up the twenty-nine of his reign. John Wesley adds the pastoral hinge on which the whole story turns: such threatenings, though absolutely expressed, "have often secret conditions," so that the sentence of death is not yet irreversible. Henry gathers up the temporal and the spiritual together, bidding a man set the heart in order by repentance and faith, and, if not done before, set the house in order too. The witnesses do not divide over the message, only over the malady; together they leave Hezekiah under the arrest of death, and turn the reader toward the prayer that will meet it.
2:
"And he turned his face to the wall, and prayed unto the LORD." The witnesses linger over the posture and the tears. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry offers two readings of the turned face: perhaps for privacy, since the sick king could not retire to his closet as he used, yet retired as well as he could, turning from the company about him to converse with God; or perhaps toward the temple, to show how willingly he would have gone up thither to pray, as he had done in his distress. Either way Henry draws the lesson that when we cannot pray with the usual privacy or solemnity, we must not therefore omit our devotions but compose ourselves to them as well as we can. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same more briefly: he turned his face as he lay in his bed, unable to reach his closet, but retiring as well as he could. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, compares the same gesture in 1 Kings 21:4 and suggests Hezekiah turned to avoid being disturbed in his prayer, and because grief instinctively seeks a hiding-place.

Over the weeping the witnesses weigh the king's heart. Henry allows that some gather from his sore weeping that he was unwilling to die, for it is in the nature of man to dread the separation of soul and body, and the Old-Testament saints, to whom another world was but darkly revealed, were not so willing to leave this as the New-Testament saints were. Yet there was something peculiar in Hezekiah's case: he was in the midst of his usefulness, his work of reformation begun, with as yet no son to succeed him, so that both the peace of his kingdom and the promise to David seemed in danger. Perhaps, Henry adds, these were only tears of importunity and lively affection in prayer, as Jacob wept and made supplication, and as our Saviour offered strong cries with tears to him who was able to save him (Heb. 5:7).

Keil and Delitzsch, also grammatical-historical, press the point of the tears further: Hezekiah "wept violently," not merely because he was to die without an heir, since Manasseh was not born till three years afterwards, but because he was to die in the very midst of his life, when God had promised long life to the righteous. The witnesses do not contend; they read the turned face as the reflex of a soul seeking God in extremity, and the tears as the honest grief of a man who loved both his God and the work God had given him, unwilling to be cut off with so much still undone.
3:
The prayer itself now stands open: "Remember now, O LORD, how I have walked before thee in truth and with a perfect heart." Here the witnesses genuinely divide over whether the plea breathes humble faith or a want of it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is the most uneasy. He grants that Hezekiah rightly took Isaiah's absolute words as conditionally meant, else he could not have prayed at all; but of the king's appeal to his own uprightness he asks plainly whether it was impious, and answers that, though not impious, "it certainly did not savor much either of humility or of a due sense of his own weakness." If Hezekiah had a perfect heart, who made it such but God? These, Clarke says, were the times of ignorance God winked at; the Gospel teaches a different lesson.

The grammatical-historical witnesses defend the prayer. Keil and Delitzsch insist that his appeal to walking before the Lord in truth and with a devoted heart "did not imply by any means a self-righteous trust in his own virtue, for walking before God with a thoroughly devoted heart was impossible without faith." Charles Ellicott adds that there is nothing surprising in the apparent self-praise if we recall like passages in the Psalms and in Neh. 13:14; Hezekiah simply deprecates an untimely death, the punishment of the wicked in Prov. 10:27, on the ground of his zeal for the Lord and against the idols. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry threads the needle: our own sincerity may be pleaded not as a debt that could demand a reward, for Christ's righteousness alone purchases mercy, but as the condition of the covenant which God has himself wrought in us; "it is the work of thy own hands; Lord, own it." The testimony of conscience that we have walked with God is a real support when we come to look death in the face (2 Cor. 1:12).

Over the tears the same witnesses offer differing motives. Clarke thinks Hezekiah wept because his prospects of another world were clouded, and because he longed to live to see the enemies of God overthrown; Wesley traces the weeping to the horror of death common under the Old Testament, before the grace of God in Christ was fully manifested, and to the distracted state in which church and kingdom would be left through the uncertain succession; Ellicott notes that Josephus set it down to childlessness, but dismisses that as a midrashic enlargement. Henry closes the matter with the shape of the prayer: Hezekiah does not pray "Lord, spare me," nor "Lord, take me," but "Lord, remember me — whether I live or die, let me be thine."
4:
The answer outruns the prayer. "Afore Isaiah was gone out into the middle court, the word of the LORD came to him." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the swiftness the whole point: this prayer of the godly king was answered immediately, for Isaiah had not gone out of the midst of the city when he was sent back. They read the disputed word as "the middle city," the central portion of Jerusalem, namely the Zion city in which the royal citadel stood, and treat the marginal "central court" as merely an interpretation of the same, the court of the royal castle, on the analogy of 2 Kings 10:25. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, judges "middle court" the true reading of some Hebrew manuscripts and all the versions, the Hebrew text "city" being wrong, while noting Keil's harmonizing view that "city" here denotes the castle, the royal residence.

The reformed John Gill sets out the textual choice without forcing it: the marginal reading, which our translators follow, is "the middle court" of the king's palace, the other court within the porch; the textual reading is "the middle city," Jerusalem being divided into three parts, of which this was the central. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the philology precisely: hatstser, "the court," is the Masoretic Keri; ha'ir, "the city," is the reading of the text and of most manuscripts; but the versions follow the Keri. The scribes and the ancient translators, that is, pull gently against the received consonants, and the sense hangs on whether the prophet was overtaken still within the palace or still within the town.

John Wesley, also wesleyan-arminian, waves away the difficulty and seizes the marrow: the court was that of the king's palace, and the detail is set down "to shew God's great readiness to hear the prayers of his children." Here the witnesses converge. Whether Isaiah had crossed the middle court or was still in the central city, the striking thing is that the reversal came before he could quit the precincts at all, the sentence of death recalled almost before the messenger who carried it was out of earshot. The textual haze over a single word cannot dim the mercy it frames: that God, who had just said "thou shalt die," interrupts his own prophet on the threshold to say that Hezekiah shall live.
5:
"Turn again, and tell Hezekiah the captain of my people... I have heard thy prayer, I have seen thy tears: behold, I will heal thee." The witnesses first weigh the title. The reformed John Gill glosses "the captain of my people" simply as the king of them, following the Targum. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, renders it "ruler," the nagid, a designation of honour used of Saul and of Solomon (1 Sam. 10:1; 1 Kings 1:35), and observes that both this phrase and the closing "I will heal thee" are wanting in the parallel account, which reads like an abbreviated transcript of the present or of a common original.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds in every clause a fresh mercy. God calls Hezekiah "the captain of his people" to intimate that he would reprieve him for his people's sake, since in this time of war they could ill spare such a captain; and he calls himself "the God of David" to intimate that he would spare him out of regard to the covenant made with David and the promise to ordain a lamp for him. God honours the prayer by the very notice he takes of it, "I have heard thy prayers, I have seen thy tears," for prayers that have much life and affection in them are in a special manner pleasing to God. And then God exceeds the prayer: Hezekiah had begged only that his integrity be remembered, but God promises to heal him, to restore him so far that on the third day he should go up to the house of the LORD, to add fifteen years to his life, and to deliver the city from Assyria.

On that third-day promise the grammatical-historical witnesses dwell. Keil and Delitzsch take the added assurance, "on the third day thou shalt go into the house of the LORD," as a distinct pledge given to the king of the promised recovery, and note that Isaiah returned to him in consequence of a fresh divine revelation. Ellicott compares the "third day" of Hosea 6:2, but insists there is no ground here for taking it other than literally. Henry adds the tender touch that God knew Hezekiah's heart, how dearly he loved the habitation of God's house, and so promised him the very thing that would please him best, that his healed soul might live and praise its healer. The witnesses agree throughout: the answer is pure grace, addressed to a king loved for his people's sake and his father David's, and heaped up beyond all that he had dared to ask.
6:
"And I will add unto thy days fifteen years." The promise of a fixed term draws from the witnesses a rare unanimity of reflection on the mercy of not knowing our own. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes the strangeness of it: this is "the first and only man who was ever informed of the term of his life," and was this a privilege? Surely no. If Hezekiah was attached to life, as he plainly was, how must his mind be affected to mark the sinking years, feeling at the close of each that so much was cut off, so that he must "feel a thousand deaths in fearing one." Nothing, Clarke judges, would be wanting to complete the misery of men, save the place of torment, were they told the precise hour of their end; and God in abundant mercy has hidden it from their eyes. John Wesley, of the same school, lands on the identical lesson from the other side: we have not an instance of any other told beforehand just how long he should live, for "God has wisely kept us at uncertainties, that we may be always ready."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sees the gift as a renewed lease. The added years would bring Hezekiah only to some fifty-four or fifty-five, no great age, yet longer than he had lately expected; his lease, which he thought expiring, was renewed. Henry too marvels that no other man has the instance of so exact a reprieve, and draws Wesley's moral, that our uncertainty is designed to keep us watchful.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the reckoning and the rider. In the Jewish manner of counting, fourteen years and a fraction would tell as fifteen; and the added clause, "I will deliver thee and this city out of the hand of the king of Assyria," shows that the Assyrians had not yet retired from the West, so that the promise repeats the pledge already given for the Lord's own sake and his servant David's in 2 Kings 19:34. The reformed John Gill, briefer here, simply refers the fifteen years to the fuller treatment of the promise elsewhere. The witnesses gather to one confession: the reprieve is real and dated, but the very fact that its date was told to Hezekiah, and is told to no one else, becomes in their hands a sermon on the goodness of God in leaving all other men to live, and to prepare, in a wholesome ignorance of their last day.
7:
"And Isaiah said, Take a lump of figs. And they took and laid it on the boil, and he recovered." The witnesses read the cure as the meeting of a plain remedy with a plain miracle. The reformed John Gill notes that these were not moist but dried figs, a pressed cake, and therefore "the less likely to have any effect in curing the boil," the homeliness of the means throwing the wonder into relief. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns physician: the Hebrew shechin denotes any inflammatory tumour, boil, or abscess, and the versions render it sore or wound; what exactly the malady was cannot be said, some guessing a pleurisy, some the plague, some the elephantiasis, some a quinsy. A poultice of figs, he grants, is medically proper "to maturate a boil, or to discuss any obstinate inflammatory swelling," as Pliny observed of such applications; yet this was simply the natural means God chose to bless, and without his interposition Hezekiah must have died.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes Isaiah the king's physician and draws the practical divinity out at length. The prophet ordered an outward application, a very cheap and common thing, that might contribute something to the cure; and yet, considering to what a height the disease had come and how suddenly it was checked, the healing was no less than miraculous. From this Henry takes two rules: that it is our duty when sick to use such means as are proper to help nature, else we do not trust God but tempt him; and that plain and ordinary medicines must not be despised, for many such God has graciously made serviceable to man. What God appoints, he will bless and make effectual.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add only the brief clinical note that Isaiah ordered the lump of figs laid upon the boil, and Hezekiah revived again. So the witnesses stand together, none of them setting the medicine against the miracle. The figs were real figs and did the work figs do; but the speed of the recovery, on a disease already mortal, betrays the hand that both sends and remands our diseases at his pleasure. The lesson the witnesses press is the balance of faith and prudence: to reach for the remedy, and to look past it to the Lord who heals, refusing either to despise the means or to rest in them.
8:
"What shall be the sign that the LORD will heal me, and that I shall go up into the house of the LORD the third day?" The witnesses ask why a man just promised his life should seek a further token, and answer that it was faith reaching for certainty. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke says Hezekiah wished to be fully convinced that his cure was to be entirely supernatural, and so "seeks one miracle to prove the truth of the other, that nothing might remain equivocal." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the request beside the earlier sign in 2 Kings 19:29 and beside Isaiah's offer to Ahaz in Isa. 7:11, reading the sign as obviously a token that the prophet's word would come true.

That comparison with Ahaz gives the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry his key. Hezekiah begged a sign not from distrust of God's power or promise, but because he looked on the thing promised as very great and worthy to be so confirmed, and because it had been usual with God thus to favour his people. Above all, Henry notes, he remembered how much God was displeased with his own father Ahaz for refusing to ask a sign; and it is a fine irony, which the coming verses will sharpen, that the token should be given on the very dial that bore Ahaz's name. And Henry marks the direction of the king's desire: he asked what should be the sign not that he should go up to the throne or the seat of judgment, but "up to the house of the LORD," for he wished to recover only that he might glorify God in the gates of Zion, since it is not worth while to live for any other purpose than to serve him.

The reformed John Gill observes that Hezekiah spoke, or had spoken, before the plaster of figs was so much as laid on, as soon as he was told he should be healed, his faith outrunning the remedy. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, turn to the grammar of Isaiah's reply, where the shadow's going forward or back is put as a question over a perfect verb, and reject the smoother rendering that would make the prophet simply offer an alternative, since it cannot be reconciled with the Hebrew as it stands. The witnesses thus hold together the pastoral and the philological: a king who asks a sign not to doubt but to worship, and a text whose very wording sets up the strange choice of the next verse.
9:
"This sign shalt thou have of the LORD... shall the shadow go forward ten degrees, or go back ten degrees?" The reformed John Gill takes the plain sense first: the sign was to assure the king that the LORD would do as he had spoken and cure him, so that he might go up to the temple on the third day. But the verse is chiefly a field for the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, who reads the Hebrew with great care and some suspicion. At first glance, he says, the text seems to state rather than to ask, "the shadow hath marched ten steps; shall it return ten steps?", yet Hezekiah's answer implies a real alternative, and the Septuagint, Targum, Vulgate, and Syriac each handle the clause differently, so that the Hebrew is very probably corrupt. He weighs emendations: reading the first word as an infinitive on the analogy of 2 Kings 19:29, or as a question, "shall it go up?", after the hint of the Vulgate.

On the instrument itself Ellicott is expansive. The word rendered "degrees" means "steps" or "stairs" wherever it occurs, as in Exod. 20:26 and the steps of Solomon's throne in 1 Kings 10:20 and the stairs of Neh. 3:15; hence the probability in Knobel's conjecture that "the dial of Ahaz" was a column rising from a circular flight of steps, casting the shadow of its top on the highest step at noon and on the lowest at morning and evening. Such a device, set up in the palace court, would be visible to Hezekiah as he lay facing the window. Ellicott recalls that Herodotus ascribes the invention of the gnomon to the Babylonians, and that the inscriptions show they divided the day into periods each subdivided into sixty parts.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, confirm the grammatical crux, rendering the prophet's words as the offer of a hard choice and rejecting the older Latin construction that cannot stand against the perfect verb. The witnesses do not quarrel over the substance, a shadow to be moved ten steps as a pledge of healing, but they leave the reader aware that the very shape of the sentence, and the make of the dial on which it turned, were matters the ancient translators already found puzzling. What is clear is the offer: the ordinary or the impossible, forward or back, laid before a dying king for his choosing.
10:
Offered the choice, Hezekiah asks the harder thing. "It is a light thing for the shadow to go down ten degrees: nay, but let the shadow return backward ten degrees." The reformed John Gill explains the reasoning: it was comparatively a light thing for the shadow to go forward, for that was agreeable to the nature and course of the sun, and so the miracle would be less apparent; to go down ten degrees all at once would itself be extraordinary, but the backward motion would be unmistakable. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott says the same, that the forward course seemed easy "because that was the ordinary course of things," though as a natural phenomenon the sudden lengthening of the shadow would have been quite as wonderful as its retreat; what is in any way familiar seems easier than what has never been seen. He notes that the verb rendered "go down" is better "spread."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley weighs the two motions like a physicist: for the sun to go down, that is forward, in an instant would be natural for the kind of motion though miraculous for its swiftness, "but the other would be both ways miraculous," against nature in direction as well as in speed. Hezekiah, choosing the doubly miraculous sign, sought the plainest possible seal on the promise.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds a further fitness in the king's choice. He humbly desired the sun might go back because, though either motion would be a great miracle, its going back would seem the more strange, and would be the more significant of his own case, of his "returning to the days of his youth," as Elihu speaks in Job 33:25, and of the lengthening out of the day of his life. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, look ahead to the answer of the next verse, where Isaiah prays and the LORD turns the shadow back upon the sun-dial of Ahaz, which they take to be not a stair but a true gnomon such as Babylonia knew. The witnesses agree without strain: the king asked for the sign that nature could least counterfeit, and in asking it read his own recovery, a life bent backward toward youth, in the shadow bent backward on the dial.
11:
"And Isaiah the prophet cried unto the LORD: and he brought the shadow ten degrees backward." Here the witnesses part company, not over the fact but over the manner of the wonder. The reformed John Gill notes only that Isaiah prayed, and prayed earnestly, that Hezekiah's desire might be granted. But how the shadow returned is the question, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the whole spread of opinion. The historian, he says, plainly regards the sign as directly involving the divine agency, that is, as a miracle; men have explained it by a mock sun, a cloud of vapour, an earthquake, a contrivance on the dial; the church fathers, with Ephrem Syrus, believed the sun receded in his celestial path, though the text says not the sun but the shadow returned, the "sun returned" of Isa. 38:8 being only a natural way of speaking. Keil assumes a wondrous refraction of the sun's rays effected by God at the prophet's prayer, in which some later scholars concur, taking the refraction to be local only; while Thenius, after long argument for an eclipse, falls back on a merely mythical view. The fact, Ellicott concludes, is certain, due to the direct agency of Him who orders all things, but how it was effected the narrative does not disclose.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke commits himself where Ellicott suspends judgment, proposing that the miracle was wrought by refraction: since a ray of light is bent in passing through a dense medium, and the atmosphere already lifts the rising and setting sun into view before and after its true place, God might by dense clouds or vapours have refracted the light so as to throw the shadow back, without disturbing the course of the earth or the heavenly bodies. John Wesley, of the same school, takes the older and simpler view outright: the degrees were lines on the dial, and "the sun itself went back, and the shadow with it." He adds the detail that binds this verse to the next, that the miracle was noted by the Babylonians, who, learning it was done for Hezekiah's sake, sent to enquire into its truth and manner (2 Chron. 32:31); and that the dial of Ahaz stood in the king's palace, likely visible from his chamber, where the miracle could be soonest verified. So the witnesses divide honestly, whether by a refraction of the light, or a real reversal of the sun, or a manner left wholly unknown, while agreeing that the shadow truly moved, at the word of a praying prophet, as God's own seal upon a promise.
12:
"At that time Berodach-baladan... king of Babylon, sent letters and a present unto Hezekiah: for he had heard that Hezekiah had been sick." The witnesses first settle the name, then probe the motive. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agree that "Berodach" is properly Merodach, the mem and beth being easily interchanged, as the Septuagint, Syriac, and the parallel in Isa. 39:1 confirm. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, grounds the correction in the cuneiform Marduk-abla-iddina, and unfolds the man's history, his throne at the head of the Persian Gulf, his shifting struggle with Sargon and Sennacherib, before drawing the point that matters: whatever the ostensible errand, the real object was to ascertain the extent of Hezekiah's resources and to secure his alliance against the common Assyrian enemy.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, reach the same verdict. The arrival falls after Sennacherib's overthrow, at least half a year after the recovery; the pretext was congratulation on the king's health, with the further curiosity, named in the Chronicles, about the miracle of the sun-dial; but the true aim, as Josephus saw, was to make sure of Hezekiah's friendship for a coming revolt against Assyria. John Wesley agrees, taking Berodach for a Babylonian viceroy who had usurped sovereignty, and reading the embassy as sent partly for the reasons in the Chronicles and partly "to assure himself of the assistance of Hezekiah against the Assyrians, their common enemy."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry alone adds a second, religious motive beside the political. The kings of Babylon had hitherto been tributaries of Assyria (2 Kings 17:24), but this one was setting up for himself; and he courted Hezekiah upon a double account. Upon the account of religion: the Babylonians worshipped the sun, and perceiving what honour their god had done Hezekiah in going back for his sake, they thought themselves bound to honour him too, for "it is good having those our friends whom we perceive to be the favourites of heaven." And upon the account of civil interest: in answer to whose prayers heaven had dealt the fatal blow to Assyria, so that no ally could be more valuable than one with such interest in the upper world. The witnesses converge on the political design, but Henry's reading of the sun-worshippers doing homage to the God-favoured king gives the embassy a theological edge the others leave untouched, and sets up the pride by which Hezekiah will turn a courtesy into a snare.
13:
"And Hezekiah hearkened unto them, and shewed them all the house of his precious things... there was nothing in his house, nor in all his dominion, that Hezekiah shewed them not." The witnesses agree first on a textual slip and then on a sin. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, judge "hearkened" a copyist's error for "was glad of them," the reading of many codices, the versions, and the parallel; in his delight at the honour Hezekiah threw open his treasure-house, the silver and gold and spices and precious oil and all his armoury. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke concurs on the reading and sharpens the charge: the king showed all "through a spirit of folly and exultation," no doubt including the things in the house of the LORD, and in this, as the Chronicles say, God left him to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart, a trial proving there was in it "little else than pride and folly." John Wesley, of the same school, traces the ostentation to its root, that Hezekiah was lifted up by the great honour God had done him and by the respect of so mighty a monarch, and so displayed the treasures he had reserved through the Assyrian war and the spoils and presents that had since flowed in (2 Chron. 32:25).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the entertainment as courtesy overrun by vanity. It was Hezekiah's duty to be civil to the ambassadors, but he exceeded, and was "courteous to a fault," too fond of them, too forward to show them his palace and magazines, that they might report what a great king he was. And here Henry finds the deeper fault: it is not said that he showed them the temple, or the book of the law, or the manner of his worship, to win them to the true religion, though he had a fair opportunity; in complaisance he waived all that, and showed the rich furniture of his closet instead. What is more common, or seemingly more innocent, than to show friends our houses and possessions? But done in the pride of the heart, to gain applause from men and not to give praise to God, it turns into sin.

Charles Ellicott raises the one real puzzle. The mention of silver and gold seems to contradict the earlier notice that Hezekiah had emptied his treasury to Sennacherib (2 Kings 18:15); and here the critics divide. Schrader, with Robertson Smith, infers that the embassy and the sickness fall before Sennacherib's invasion; Thenius supposes instead that Hezekiah had given up only his ready money and even stripped the temple gold to feign exhaustion, keeping his real treasures hidden, and that fresh gifts poured in after the Assyrian's retreat. The witnesses leave the chronology contested, but the moral undivided: a king who had lately turned his face to the wall in prayer now turns it to a heathen embassy in pride, and the display he is so pleased with will become the measure of his loss.
14:
"Then came Isaiah the prophet unto king Hezekiah, and said unto him, What said these men? and from whence came they unto thee?" The witnesses watch the prophet turn examiner. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks the reversal of his office: Isaiah, who had so often been Hezekiah's comforter, is now his reprover, for "the blessed Spirit is both" (John 16:7-8), and ministers must be both as there is occasion. He speaks in God's name, and so calls the king to account with authority: who are these? whence come they? what have they seen? And Hezekiah, to his credit, does not resent the scrutiny nor ask why the prophet should concern himself, but submits to be questioned.

The grammatical-historical witnesses read the exchange as a probing of the heart. Keil and Delitzsch observe that Isaiah's questions were not asked for information but "to lead the king to give expression to the thoughts of his heart"; and in the answer, "They are come from a far country, even from Babylon," the king's vanity at the great honour paid him comes clearly to light. That a mighty and distant power should send to little Judah was precisely the flattery that had turned his head. Charles Ellicott hears in Isaiah's abrupt challenge the prophet's "fearless assumption of a superior position," the same we meet elsewhere in his dealings with kings, and reads it as of a piece with his settled conviction that the LORD's will is opposed to all coquetting with foreign powers (Isa. 30:1). He notes, with a touch of irony, that "far country" is the very phrase the Assyrian kings themselves used of distant Palestine.

The scene, brief as it is, turns wholly on that word "Babylon." Isaiah does not yet pronounce the sentence; he only draws it out of the king's own mouth, letting Hezekiah name the place from which his visitors came and, in naming it, betray how much he had gloried in their coming. The witnesses agree that the prophet's method is as instructive as his message: he does not accuse before he has let the man reveal himself, so that when the judgment falls in the next verses it falls on a confession already freely made. The king who had shown his treasures to strangers now, unwittingly, shows his heart to the prophet, and the far country he boasts of will prove the very land to which all he boasted of shall be carried.
15:
"And he said, What have they seen in thine house? And Hezekiah answered, All the things that are in mine house have they seen: there is nothing among my treasures that I have not shewed them." The witnesses take the king's frank answer as both honest and self-condemning. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, let the confession stand as the ground of the sentence that follows: Hezekiah admits without reserve that he has hidden nothing, and upon that admission the prophet builds his word that all these very things shall one day be carried to Babylon, the sin of vanity to be punished by the loss of what his heart was proud of.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the confession the occasion of a searching reflection. He commends its openness, that Hezekiah "made an ingenuous confession: There is nothing among my treasures that I have not shown them," but then presses the question that the king's own words provoke. Why did he not bring the ambassadors to Isaiah, and show them the prophet, who was without doubt the best treasure he had in all his dominions, and who by his prayers and prophecies had wrought the very wonders those envoys had come to enquire after? Henry hopes Hezekiah still valued Isaiah as he had in his distress; but it would have become him to prove it by presenting the ambassadors to the man of God in the first place, which might well have prevented the false step he took. He showed them all his house, and left unshown the one thing worth their seeing.

There the two witnesses meet. The very completeness of the display, "nothing... that I have not shewed them," is what turns it from hospitality into folly: a king so eager to spread out his silver and armour before strangers, yet silent about the God who had lengthened his life and turned back the sun, and silent about the prophet through whom that God had spoken. The confession is exact, and its very exactness measures the omission. Hezekiah keeps back nothing of his wealth and offers nothing of his faith; and so his open answer to Isaiah's plain question becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the hinge on which the whole embassy swings from honour to judgment, the treasures now catalogued for the prophet's ear being the same that shall presently be catalogued for the spoiler's hand.
17:
"Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house... shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left, saith the LORD." The witnesses read the sentence as pride punished in kind, and pause over its startling foresight. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the fulfilment in the latter days of the Jewish kings, when the Babylonians led the people captive and stripped land and temple alike, and points forward to Dan. 1:1-3. John Wesley, of the same school, names the ground of the judgment: it fell on Hezekiah for his pride, for his ingratitude in taking to himself the honour he should have given wholly to God, and for his carnal confidence in the league he had now struck with Babylon, by which he seemed to think his mountain so strong it could not be moved.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the irony and the justice. The treasures Hezekiah was so proud of should become a prey, and the very king of Babylon whose alliance he so courted should be the enemy to seize them; "it is just with God to take that from us which we make the matter of our pride and in which we put our confidence." Not that this display was itself the cause of the captivity, for the sins of Manasseh would be that, but it is foretold now to convince Hezekiah of the folly of his pride: the snake he is cherishing will one day sting the bosom that warms it, and his royal seed shall serve the king of Babylon, so that the wise and happy come out from her (Rev. 18:4).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the sharpest point of all, the prophecy's own tone. A more terrible irony can hardly be conceived: thy friends will prove robbers, thine allies will become thy conquerors. And that Isaiah should foresee Assyria, then in the heyday of its power, dethroned by that very Babylon which the Assyrian arms were at the moment menacing, this, Ellicott says, "can only be accepted as true by those who accept the reality of supernatural prediction." He records the naturalizing alternative of Thenius, that an Isaiah might well perceive what fate threatened little Judah in a revolution of affairs brought about by the Babylonians; but he answers that the tone of the oracle is not hypothetical but entirely positive, and that Isaiah plainly did not expect Merodach-baladan's own revolt to succeed. So the witnesses divide over the nature of the foresight, whether genuine prediction or shrewd statecraft, while agreeing on its substance: the wealth paraded before Babylon is pledged, by the word of the LORD, to Babylon's hand.
18:
"And of thy sons that shall issue from thee, which thou shalt beget, shall they take away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon." The witnesses agree on the reach of the threat and debate only the force of a single word. On the "sons," the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley concur that not the immediate children but the descendants are meant, Wesley glossing it "thy grand-children," and both point to the fulfilment when Hezekiah's line was carried into Babylon (Dan. 1:3), Ellicott adding that Ewald would include even the captivity of Hezekiah's own son Manasseh (2 Chron. 33:11).

On "eunuchs" the witnesses lean, almost together, away from the harshest sense. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, render the word sarisim as chamberlains or courtiers, "not necessarily eunuchs," comparing its use in 1 Sam. 8:15. Ellicott agrees it is rather "courtiers, palace attendants," as Josephus took it, and as Cheyne renders "chamberlains." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke likewise suggests the phrase may mean no more than that they should become household servants to the kings of Babylon, and points to the fulfilment in the stripping of the palace at 2 Kings 24:13-15.

Yet John Wesley will not let the softer rendering empty the doom of its horror. Whether called eunuchs or servants, he says, they shall be in bondage to a heathen monarch, so that "both their bodies will be subject to slavery, and their souls exposed to the peril of idolatry, and all sorts of wickedness." There the witnesses gather the verse's weight. The point is not merely a philological one about court office; it is that the children a king begets to sit upon David's throne shall instead wait in a pagan palace, their royalty spent in another's service and their faith set in the way of temptation. The treasures of the house go first (v.17), and then the sons of the house after them, the whole inheritance, gold and blood alike, drawn away to Babylon. The prophecy that began with the plunder of things ends with the captivity of persons, and it is this second stroke, falling on his own seed, that the witnesses feel Hezekiah would find the most bitter to foreknow.
19:
"Good is the word of the LORD which thou hast spoken. And he said, Is it not good, if peace and truth be in my days?" Over this reply the witnesses genuinely divide, some hearing humble submission and one hearing a note of self-interest. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the first clause as an expression of submission to the will of the Lord, like Eli's "It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good" (1 Sam. 3:18); and the second clause, spoken after a pause, as a candid acknowledgment of the pure goodness of God, that there should be peace and truth in his days at all. Charles Ellicott agrees it is pious acquiescence, finding in the prophetic word an element of mercy in that Hezekiah himself is spared, and rendering the phrase "peace and permanence," or security, as in Jer. 33:6.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses split. John Wesley reads only submission and penitence: Hezekiah "heartily submits to this sentence, as being both just and merciful," and true penitents under divine rebukes call them not only just but good, accepting the punishment of their iniquity, so that by this very acceptance he showed himself humbled for the pride of his heart. But Adam Clarke hears something less selfless. Granting that the king owned "he has spoken right, I have done foolishly, I submit," Clarke thinks the following question "seems to be rather of an interested nature," since Hezekiah does not appear to deplore the calamities coming on the land, "provided peace and truth might prevail in his days."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry stands between, defending the king yet naming the danger Clarke smells. He lays it down that Hezekiah rightly calls the word good even though it is a threatening, for every word of God is good and works for good; and that he does well to take notice of what in it is for him, that he should not live to see the evil. It is good, Henry allows, if peace and truth be in our days, if we have the true religion protected and enjoy it unmolested. Yet he adds the check: we should not be unconcerned for posterity, for it is a grief to foresee evils; the deferring of judgment is a mercy, but charity that begins at home must not end there. So the witnesses circle a real tension in the king's words, resignation shading toward complacency, without wholly resolving it: Wesley and Keil crown the reply as faith, Clarke suspects a self-regard in it, and Henry owns both the goodness Hezekiah confesses and the posterity he too easily lets go.
20:
"And the rest of the acts of Hezekiah, and all his might, and how he made a pool, and a conduit, and brought water into the city." The closing formula sends the witnesses to the king's public works and to a last moral. The reformed John Gill refers "all his might" to what he exerted both in his wars with his enemies and in the reformation of religion and the abolition of idolatry. The grammatical-historical witnesses attend to the waterworks. Keil and Delitzsch note the basin and the aqueduct by which he brought water into the city, referring back to the works about the conduit in 2 Kings 18:17; Charles Ellicott identifies the pool with the Birket-Hammam-el-Batrak and points to the fuller account of the upper watercourse of Gihon in 2 Chron. 32:30. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the engineering a mercy: to have water in plenty, at hand and convenient, without striving for it or being terrified in the drawing of it, is a great blessing, for the want of water would be a great calamity.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns from the water to the wound. He gathers from the Chronicles what this book omits, Hezekiah's pride, his storehouses and riches (2 Chron. 32:27), and then reads the Babylonian embassy as the seed of the nation's ruin. When those foreigners had seen such profusion of wealth and so fruitful a land, it was natural for them to covet it, to report it, and at last to project the conquest of Jerusalem. Clarke reaches for history to prove the pattern: as the Goths and Vandals, seeing the pleasant plains and treasures of Italy, never rested till their swords possessed it; as the Saxons, invited by Vortigern, saw that the land was good and seized it; as the Danes did after them in England. To be lifted up with pride in any blessing, he concludes, is the surest way to lose it, for God resumes what our vanity abuses; the Jews fell to Babylon as the very political consequence of Hezekiah's ostentation, and "the judge of all the earth will do right."

So the witnesses close the reign on a double note. Gill and Henry and the two grammarians dwell on the good Hezekiah did, the reformer who cleansed the worship and the builder who brought living water through the rock into his besieged city. Clarke will not let the reader forget the folly that shadowed the greatness, turning the very abundance the conduit secured into a lure for the spoiler. The chronicle that records the pool records also the pride; and the water Hezekiah led into Jerusalem outlasted him, while the treasures he showed to Babylon drew Babylon down upon his sons.
21:
"And Hezekiah slept with his fathers: and Manasseh his son reigned in his stead." The witnesses give the death-notice its due honour and its due sorrow. The reformed John Gill observes that this book records only that he "died, as they did," naming no burial-place, but that the Chronicles supply the honour paid him: he was buried in the chief of the sepulchres of the sons of David, and the inhabitants of Judah and Jerusalem did him honour at his death by a great concourse, by burning spices for him, and by a public mourning of appointed length (2 Chron. 32:33). The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add only the bare succession, that Hezekiah slept with his fathers and Manasseh his son reigned in his stead.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds the whole weight of the verse in that son. Here the historian leaves the good king "asleep with his fathers, and a son in his throne that proved very untoward; for parents cannot give grace to their children." Henry lingers on the strange alternation of the house of Judah: wicked Ahaz was the son of a godly father and the father of a godly son, while holy Hezekiah was the son of a wicked father and the father of a wicked son, the dark reign of Manasseh opening at 2 Kings 21:1. Grace, that is, does not run in the blood; the best of fathers cannot entail his piety as he entails his crown.

Yet Henry will not end in mere lament. When the land was not reformed as it should have been by a good reign, he says, it was plagued and ripened for ruin by a bad one; and yet even then it was tried again with a good one, that it might appear how loath God was to cut off his people. So the witnesses lay Hezekiah to rest with a sober symmetry. The reformer who turned his face to the wall and was heard, who saw the shadow drawn back and the Assyrian broken, passes into the sepulchres of David honoured and mourned; but the throne descends to a son who will undo his reformation, and the reader is left to feel both the limits of the best king's legacy and the patience of the God who, reign after reign, would not yet make an end. Thus the story of Hezekiah closes, a life lengthened by prayer, a name honoured in death, and a succession that turns at once to the night.
